content.content:
1→# Anatomy of a Skill - Understanding the Structure
     2→
     3→**Want to understand how skills work under the hood?** This guide breaks down every part of a skill file.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## 📁 Basic Folder Structure
     8→
     9→```
    10→skills/
    11→└── my-skill-name/
    12→    ├── SKILL.md              ← Required: The main skill definition
    13→    ├── examples/             ← Optional: Example files
    14→    │   ├── example1.js
    15→    │   └── example2.py
    16→    ├── scripts/              ← Optional: Helper scripts
    17→    │   └── helper.sh
    18→    ├── templates/            ← Optional: Code templates
    19→    │   └── template.tsx
    20→    ├── references/           ← Optional: Reference documentation
    21→    │   └── api-docs.md
    22→    └── README.md             ← Optional: Additional documentation
    23→```
    24→
    25→**Key Rule:** Only `SKILL.md` is required. Everything else is optional!
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## SKILL.md Structure
    30→
    31→Every `SKILL.md` file has two main parts:
    32→
    33→### 1. Frontmatter (Metadata)
    34→
    35→### 2. Content (Instructions)
    36→
    37→Let's break down each part:
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## Part 1: Frontmatter
    42→
    43→The frontmatter is at the very top, wrapped in `---`:
    44→
    45→```markdown
    46→---
    47→name: my-skill-name
    48→description: "Brief description of what this skill does"
    49→---
    50→```
    51→
    52→### Required Fields
    53→
    54→#### `name`
    55→
    56→- **What it is:** The skill's identifier
    57→- **Format:** lowercase-with-hyphens
    58→- **Must match:** The folder name exactly
    59→- **Example:** `stripe-integration`
    60→
    61→#### `description`
    62→
    63→- **What it is:** One-sentence summary
    64→- **Format:** String in quotes
    65→- **Length:** Keep it under 150 characters
    66→- **Example:** `"Stripe payment integration patterns including checkout, subscriptions, and webhooks"`
    67→
    68→### Optional Fields
    69→
    70→Some skills include additional metadata:
    71→
    72→```markdown
    73→---
    74→name: my-skill-name
    75→description: "Brief description"
    76→risk: "safe" # none | safe | critical | offensive (see QUALITY_BAR.md)
    77→source: "community"
    78→tags: ["react", "typescript"]
    79→---
    80→```
    81→
    82→---
    83→
    84→## Part 2: Content
    85→
    86→After the frontmatter comes the actual skill content. Here's the recommended structure:
    87→
    88→### Recommended Sections
    89→
    90→#### 1. Title (H1)
    91→
    92→```markdown
    93→# Skill Title
    94→```
    95→
    96→- Use a clear, descriptive title
    97→- Usually matches or expands on the skill name
    98→
    99→#### 2. Overview
   100→
   101→```markdown
   102→## Overview
   103→
   104→A brief explanation of what this skill does and why it exists.
   105→2-4 sentences is perfect.
   106→```
   107→
   108→#### 3. When to Use
   109→
   110→```markdown
   111→## When to Use This Skill
   112→
   113→- Use when you need to [scenario 1]
   114→- Use when working with [scenario 2]
   115→- Use when the user asks about [scenario 3]
   116→```
   117→
   118→**Why this matters:** Helps the AI know when to activate this skill
   119→
   120→#### 4. Core Instructions
   121→
   122→```markdown
   123→## How It Works
   124→
   125→### Step 1: [Action]
   126→
   127→Detailed instructions...
   128→
   129→### Step 2: [Action]
   130→
   131→More instructions...
   132→```
   133→
   134→**This is the heart of your skill** - clear, actionable steps
   135→
   136→#### 5. Examples
   137→
   138→```markdown
   139→## Examples
   140→
   141→### Example 1: [Use Case]
   142→
   143→\`\`\`javascript
   144→// Example code
   145→\`\`\`
   146→
   147→### Example 2: [Another Use Case]
   148→
   149→\`\`\`javascript
   150→// More code
   151→\`\`\`
   152→```
   153→
   154→**Why examples matter:** They show the AI exactly what good output looks like
   155→
   156→#### 6. Best Practices
   157→
   158→```markdown
   159→## Best Practices
   160→
   161→- ✅ Do this
   162→- ✅ Also do this
   163→- ❌ Don't do this
   164→- ❌ Avoid this
   165→```
   166→
   167→#### 7. Common Pitfalls
   168→
   169→```markdown
   170→## Common Pitfalls
   171→
   172→- **Problem:** Description
   173→  **Solution:** How to fix it
   174→```
   175→
   176→#### 8. Related Skills
   177→
   178→```markdown
   179→## Related Skills
   180→
   181→- `@other-skill` - When to use this instead
   182→- `@complementary-skill` - How this works together
   183→```
   184→
   185→---
   186→
   187→## Writing Effective Instructions
   188→
   189→### Use Clear, Direct Language
   190→
   191→**❌ Bad:**
   192→
   193→```markdown
   194→You might want to consider possibly checking if the user has authentication.
   195→```
   196→
   197→**✅ Good:**
   198→
   199→```markdown
   200→Check if the user is authenticated before proceeding.
   201→```
   202→
   203→### Use Action Verbs
   204→
   205→**❌ Bad:**
   206→
   207→```markdown
   208→The file should be created...
   209→```
   210→
   211→**✅ Good:**
   212→
   213→```markdown
   214→Create the file...
   215→```
   216→
   217→### Be Specific
   218→
   219→**❌ Bad:**
   220→
   221→```markdown
   222→Set up the database properly.
   223→```
   224→
   225→**✅ Good:**
   226→
   227→```markdown
   228→1. Create a PostgreSQL database
   229→2. Run migrations: `npm run migrate`
   230→3. Seed initial data: `npm run seed`
   231→```
   232→
   233→---
   234→
   235→## Optional Components
   236→
   237→### Scripts Directory
   238→
   239→If your skill needs helper scripts:
   240→
   241→```
   242→scripts/
   243→├── setup.sh          ← Setup automation
   244→├── validate.py       ← Validation tools
   245→└── generate.js       ← Code generators
   246→```
   247→
   248→**Reference them in SKILL.md:**
   249→
   250→```markdown
   251→Run the setup script:
   252→\`\`\`bash
   253→bash scripts/setup.sh
   254→\`\`\`
   255→```
   256→
   257→### Examples Directory
   258→
   259→Real-world examples that demonstrate the skill:
   260→
   261→```
   262→examples/
   263→├── basic-usage.js
   264→├── advanced-pattern.ts
   265→└── full-implementation/
   266→    ├── index.js
   267→    └── config.json
   268→```
   269→
   270→### Templates Directory
   271→
   272→Reusable code templates:
   273→
   274→```
   275→templates/
   276→├── component.tsx
   277→├── test.spec.ts
   278→└── config.json
   279→```
   280→
   281→**Reference in SKILL.md:**
   282→
   283→```markdown
   284→Use this template as a starting point:
   285→\`\`\`typescript
   286→{{#include templates/component.tsx}}
   287→\`\`\`
   288→```
   289→
   290→### References Directory
   291→
   292→External documentation or API references:
   293→
   294→```
   295→references/
   296→├── api-docs.md
   297→├── best-practices.md
   298→└── troubleshooting.md
   299→```
   300→
   301→---
   302→
   303→## Skill Size Guidelines
   304→
   305→### Minimum Viable Skill
   306→
   307→- **Frontmatter:** name + description
   308→- **Content:** 100-200 words
   309→- **Sections:** Overview + Instructions
   310→
   311→### Standard Skill
   312→
   313→- **Frontmatter:** name + description
   314→- **Content:** 300-800 words
   315→- **Sections:** Overview + When to Use + Instructions + Examples
   316→
   317→### Comprehensive Skill
   318→
   319→- **Frontmatter:** name + description + optional fields
   320→- **Content:** 800-2000 words
   321→- **Sections:** All recommended sections
   322→- **Extras:** Scripts, examples, templates
   323→
   324→**Rule of thumb:** Start small, expand based on feedback
   325→
   326→---
   327→
   328→## Formatting Best Practices
   329→
   330→### Use Markdown Effectively
   331→
   332→#### Code Blocks
   333→
   334→Always specify the language:
   335→
   336→```markdown
   337→\`\`\`javascript
   338→const example = "code";
   339→\`\`\`
   340→```
   341→
   342→#### Lists
   343→
   344→Use consistent formatting:
   345→
   346→```markdown
   347→- Item 1
   348→- Item 2
   349→  - Sub-item 2.1
   350→  - Sub-item 2.2
   351→```
   352→
   353→#### Emphasis
   354→
   355→- **Bold** for important terms: `**important**`
   356→- _Italic_ for emphasis: `*emphasis*`
   357→- `Code` for commands/code: `` `code` ``
   358→
   359→#### Links
   360→
   361→```markdown
   362→[Link text](https://example.com)
   363→```
   364→
   365→---
   366→
   367→## ✅ Quality Checklist
   368→
   369→Before finalizing your skill:
   370→
   371→### Content Quality
   372→
   373→- [ ] Instructions are clear and actionable
   374→- [ ] Examples are realistic and helpful
   375→- [ ] No typos or grammar errors
   376→- [ ] Technical accuracy verified
   377→
   378→### Structure
   379→
   380→- [ ] Frontmatter is valid YAML
   381→- [ ] Name matches folder name
   382→- [ ] Sections are logically organized
   383→- [ ] Headings follow hierarchy (H1 → H2 → H3)
   384→
   385→### Completeness
   386→
   387→- [ ] Overview explains the "why"
   388→- [ ] Instructions explain the "how"
   389→- [ ] Examples show the "what"
   390→- [ ] Edge cases are addressed
   391→
   392→### Usability
   393→
   394→- [ ] A beginner could follow this
   395→- [ ] An expert would find it useful
   396→- [ ] The AI can parse it correctly
   397→- [ ] It solves a real problem
   398→
   399→---
   400→
   401→## 🔍 Real-World Example Analysis
   402→
   403→Let's analyze a real skill: `brainstorming`
   404→
   405→```markdown
   406→---
   407→name: brainstorming
   408→description: "You MUST use this before any creative work..."
   409→---
   410→```
   411→
   412→**Analysis:**
   413→
   414→- ✅ Clear name
   415→- ✅ Strong description with urgency ("MUST use")
   416→- ✅ Explains when to use it
   417→
   418→```markdown
   419→# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs
   420→
   421→## Overview
   422→
   423→Help turn ideas into fully formed designs...
   424→```
   425→
   426→**Analysis:**
   427→
   428→- ✅ Clear title
   429→- ✅ Concise overview
   430→- ✅ Explains the value proposition
   431→
   432→```markdown
   433→## The Process
   434→
   435→**Understanding the idea:**
   436→
   437→- Check out the current project state first
   438→- Ask questions one at a time
   439→```
   440→
   441→**Analysis:**
   442→
   443→- ✅ Broken into clear phases
   444→- ✅ Specific, actionable steps
   445→- ✅ Easy to follow
   446→
   447→---
   448→
   449→## Advanced Patterns
   450→
   451→### Conditional Logic
   452→
   453→```markdown
   454→## Instructions
   455→
   456→If the user is working with React:
   457→
   458→- Use functional components
   459→- Prefer hooks over class components
   460→
   461→If the user is working with Vue:
   462→
   463→- Use Composition API
   464→- Follow Vue 3 patterns
   465→```
   466→
   467→### Progressive Disclosure
   468→
   469→```markdown
   470→## Basic Usage
   471→
   472→[Simple instructions for common cases]
   473→
   474→## Advanced Usage
   475→
   476→[Complex patterns for power users]
   477→```
   478→
   479→### Cross-References
   480→
   481→```markdown
   482→## Related Workflows
   483→
   484→1. First, use `@brainstorming` to design
   485→2. Then, use `@writing-plans` to plan
   486→3. Finally, use `@test-driven-development` to implement
   487→```
   488→
   489→---
   490→
   491→## Skill Effectiveness Metrics
   492→
   493→How to know if your skill is good:
   494→
   495→### Clarity Test
   496→
   497→- Can someone unfamiliar with the topic follow it?
   498→- Are there any ambiguous instructions?
   499→
   500→### Completeness Test
   501→
   502→- Does it cover the happy path?
   503→- Does it handle edge cases?
   504→- Are error scenarios addressed?
   505→
   506→### Usefulness Test
   507→
   508→- Does it solve a real problem?
   509→- Would you use this yourself?
   510→- Does it save time or improve quality?
   511→
   512→---
   513→
   514→## Learning from Existing Skills
   515→
   516→### Study These Examples
   517→
   518→**For Beginners:**
   519→
   520→- `skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md` - Clear structure
   521→- `skills/git-pushing/SKILL.md` - Simple and focused
   522→- `skills/copywriting/SKILL.md` - Good examples
   523→
   524→**For Advanced:**
   525→

   541→

   549→Make the code better.

   551→

   557→1. Extract repeated logic into functions

   561→

   569→

   572→

   577→
   578→[Instructions without any code examples]
   579→```
   580→

   583→

   585→
   586→```markdown

   588→```
   589→

# Anatomy of a Skill - Understanding the Structure

**Want to understand how skills work under the hood?** This guide breaks down every part of a skill file.

---

## 📁 Basic Folder Structure

```
skills/
└── my-skill-name/
    ├── SKILL.md              ← Required: The main skill definition
    ├── examples/             ← Optional: Example files
    │   ├── example1.js
    │   └── example2.py
    ├── scripts/              ← Optional: Helper scripts
    │   └── helper.sh
    ├── templates/            ← Optional: Code templates
    │   └── template.tsx
    ├── references/           ← Optional: Reference documentation
    │   └── api-docs.md
    └── README.md             ← Optional: Additional documentation
```

**Key Rule:** Only `SKILL.md` is required. Everything else is optional!

---

## SKILL.md Structure

Every `SKILL.md` file has two main parts:

### 1. Frontmatter (Metadata)

### 2. Content (Instructions)

Let's break down each part:

---

## Part 1: Frontmatter

The frontmatter is at the very top, wrapped in `---`:

```markdown
---
name: my-skill-name
description: "Brief description of what this skill does"
---
```

### Required Fields

#### `name`

- **What it is:** The skill's identifier
- **Format:** lowercase-with-hyphens
- **Must match:** The folder name exactly
- **Example:** `stripe-integration`

#### `description`

- **What it is:** One-sentence summary
- **Format:** String in quotes
- **Length:** Keep it under 150 characters
- **Example:** `"Stripe payment integration patterns including checkout, subscriptions, and webhooks"`

### Optional Fields

Some skills include additional metadata:

```markdown
---
name: my-skill-name
description: "Brief description"
risk: "safe" # none | safe | critical | offensive (see QUALITY_BAR.md)
source: "community"
tags: ["react", "typescript"]
---
```

---

## Part 2: Content

After the frontmatter comes the actual skill content. Here's the recommended structure:

### Recommended Sections

#### 1. Title (H1)

```markdown
# Skill Title
```

- Use a clear, descriptive title
- Usually matches or expands on the skill name

#### 2. Overview

```markdown
## Overview

A brief explanation of what this skill does and why it exists.
2-4 sentences is perfect.
```

#### 3. When to Use

```markdown
## When to Use This Skill

- Use when you need to [scenario 1]
- Use when working with [scenario 2]
- Use when the user asks about [scenario 3]
```

**Why this matters:** Helps the AI know when to activate this skill

#### 4. Core Instructions

```markdown
## How It Works

### Step 1: [Action]

Detailed instructions...

### Step 2: [Action]

More instructions...
```

**This is the heart of your skill** - clear, actionable steps

#### 5. Examples

```markdown
## Examples

### Example 1: [Use Case]

\`\`\`javascript
// Example code
\`\`\`

### Example 2: [Another Use Case]

\`\`\`javascript
// More code
\`\`\`
```

**Why examples matter:** They show the AI exactly what good output looks like

#### 6. Best Practices

```markdown
## Best Practices

- ✅ Do this
- ✅ Also do this
- ❌ Don't do this
- ❌ Avoid this
```

#### 7. Common Pitfalls

```markdown
## Common Pitfalls

- **Problem:** Description
  **Solution:** How to fix it
```

#### 8. Related Skills

```markdown
## Related Skills

- `@other-skill` - When to use this instead
- `@complementary-skill` - How this works together
```

---

## Writing Effective Instructions

### Use Clear, Direct Language

**❌ Bad:**

```markdown
You might want to consider possibly checking if the user has authentication.
```

**✅ Good:**

```markdown
Check if the user is authenticated before proceeding.
```

### Use Action Verbs

**❌ Bad:**

```markdown
The file should be created...
```

**✅ Good:**

```markdown
Create the file...
```

### Be Specific

**❌ Bad:**

```markdown
Set up the database properly.
```

**✅ Good:**

```markdown
1. Create a PostgreSQL database
2. Run migrations: `npm run migrate`
3. Seed initial data: `npm run seed`
```

---

## Optional Components

### Scripts Directory

If your skill needs helper scripts:

```
scripts/
├── setup.sh          ← Setup automation
├── validate.py       ← Validation tools
└── generate.js       ← Code generators
```

**Reference them in SKILL.md:**

```markdown
Run the setup script:
\`\`\`bash
bash scripts/setup.sh
\`\`\`
```

### Examples Directory

Real-world examples that demonstrate the skill:

```
examples/
├── basic-usage.js
├── advanced-pattern.ts
└── full-implementation/
    ├── index.js
    └── config.json
```

### Templates Directory

Reusable code templates:

```
templates/
├── component.tsx
├── test.spec.ts
└── config.json
```

**Reference in SKILL.md:**

```markdown
Use this template as a starting point:
\`\`\`typescript
{{#include templates/component.tsx}}
\`\`\`
```

### References Directory

External documentation or API references:

```
references/
├── api-docs.md
├── best-practices.md
└── troubleshooting.md
```

---

## Skill Size Guidelines

### Minimum Viable Skill

- **Frontmatter:** name + description
- **Content:** 100-200 words
- **Sections:** Overview + Instructions

### Standard Skill

- **Frontmatter:** name + description
- **Content:** 300-800 words
- **Sections:** Overview + When to Use + Instructions + Examples

### Comprehensive Skill

- **Frontmatter:** name + description + optional fields
- **Content:** 800-2000 words
- **Sections:** All recommended sections
- **Extras:** Scripts, examples, templates

**Rule of thumb:** Start small, expand based on feedback

---

## Formatting Best Practices

### Use Markdown Effectively

#### Code Blocks

Always specify the language:

```markdown
\`\`\`javascript
const example = "code";
\`\`\`
```

#### Lists

Use consistent formatting:

```markdown
- Item 1
- Item 2
  - Sub-item 2.1
  - Sub-item 2.2
```

#### Emphasis

- **Bold** for important terms: `**important**`
- _Italic_ for emphasis: `*emphasis*`
- `Code` for commands/code: `` `code` ``

#### Links

```markdown
[Link text](https://example.com)
```

---

## ✅ Quality Checklist

Before finalizing your skill:

### Content Quality

- [ ] Instructions are clear and actionable
- [ ] Examples are realistic and helpful
- [ ] No typos or grammar errors
- [ ] Technical accuracy verified

### Structure

- [ ] Frontmatter is valid YAML
- [ ] Name matches folder name
- [ ] Sections are logically organized
- [ ] Headings follow hierarchy (H1 → H2 → H3)

### Completeness

- [ ] Overview explains the "why"
- [ ] Instructions explain the "how"
- [ ] Examples show the "what"
- [ ] Edge cases are addressed

### Usability

- [ ] A beginner could follow this
- [ ] An expert would find it useful
- [ ] The AI can parse it correctly
- [ ] It solves a real problem

---

## 🔍 Real-World Example Analysis

Let's analyze a real skill: `brainstorming`

```markdown
---
name: brainstorming
description: "You MUST use this before any creative work..."
---
```

**Analysis:**

- ✅ Clear name
- ✅ Strong description with urgency ("MUST use")
- ✅ Explains when to use it

```markdown
# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

## Overview

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs...
```

**Analysis:**

- ✅ Clear title
- ✅ Concise overview
- ✅ Explains the value proposition

```markdown
## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first
- Ask questions one at a time
```

**Analysis:**

- ✅ Broken into clear phases
- ✅ Specific, actionable steps
- ✅ Easy to follow

---

## Advanced Patterns

### Conditional Logic

```markdown
## Instructions

If the user is working with React:

- Use functional components
- Prefer hooks over class components

If the user is working with Vue:

- Use Composition API
- Follow Vue 3 patterns
```

### Progressive Disclosure

```markdown
## Basic Usage

[Simple instructions for common cases]

## Advanced Usage

[Complex patterns for power users]
```

### Cross-References

```markdown
## Related Workflows

1. First, use `@brainstorming` to design
2. Then, use `@writing-plans` to plan
3. Finally, use `@test-driven-development` to implement
```

---

## Skill Effectiveness Metrics

How to know if your skill is good:

### Clarity Test

- Can someone unfamiliar with the topic follow it?
- Are there any ambiguous instructions?

### Completeness Test

- Does it cover the happy path?
- Does it handle edge cases?
- Are error scenarios addressed?

### Usefulness Test

- Does it solve a real problem?
- Would you use this yourself?
- Does it save time or improve quality?

---

## Learning from Existing Skills

### Study These Examples

**For Beginners:**

- `skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md` - Clear structure
- `skills/git-pushing/SKILL.md` - Simple and focused
- `skills/copywriting/SKILL.md` - Good examples

**For Advanced:**

- `skills/systematic-debugging/SKILL.md` - Comprehensive
- `skills/react-best-practices/SKILL.md` - Multiple files
- `skills/loki-mode/SKILL.md` - Complex workflows

---

## 💡 Pro Tips

1. **Start with the "When to Use" section** - This clarifies the skill's purpose
2. **Write examples first** - They help you understand what you're teaching
3. **Test with an AI** - See if it actually works before submitting
4. **Get feedback** - Ask others to review your skill
5. **Iterate** - Skills improve over time based on usage

---

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

### ❌ Mistake 1: Too Vague

```markdown
## Instructions

Make the code better.
```

**✅ Fix:**

```markdown
## Instructions

1. Extract repeated logic into functions
2. Add error handling for edge cases
3. Write unit tests for core functionality
```

### ❌ Mistake 2: Too Complex

```markdown
## Instructions

[5000 words of dense technical jargon]
```

**✅ Fix:**
Break into multiple skills or use progressive disclosure

### ❌ Mistake 3: No Examples

```markdown
## Instructions

[Instructions without any code examples]
```

**✅ Fix:**
Add at least 2-3 realistic examples

### ❌ Mistake 4: Outdated Information

```markdown
Use React class components...
```

**✅ Fix:**
Keep skills updated with current best practices

---

## 🎯 Next Steps

1. **Read 3-5 existing skills** to see different styles
2. **Try the skill template** from CONTRIBUTING.md
3. **Create a simple skill** for something you know well
4. **Test it** with your AI assistant
5. **Share it** via Pull Request

---

**Remember:** Every expert was once a beginner. Start simple, learn from feedback, and improve over time! 🚀

# Getting Started with Antigravity Awesome Skills (V4)

**New here? This guide will help you supercharge your AI Agent in 5 minutes.**

---

## 🤔 What Are "Skills"?

AI Agents (like **Claude Code**, **Gemini**, **Cursor**) are smart, but they lack specific knowledge about your tools.
**Skills** are specialized instruction manuals (markdown files) that teach your AI how to perform specific tasks perfectly, every time.

**Analogy:** Your AI is a brilliant intern. **Skills** are the SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) that make them a Senior Engineer.

---

## ⚡️ Quick Start: The "Starter Packs"

Don't panic about the 626+ skills. You don't need them all at once.
We have curated **Starter Packs** to get you running immediately.

You **install the full repo once** (npx or clone); Starter Packs are curated lists to help you **pick which skills to use** by role (e.g. Web Wizard, Hacker Pack)—they are not a different way to install.

### 1. Install the Repo

**Option A — npx (easiest):**

```bash
npx antigravity-awesome-skills
```

This clones to `~/.agent/skills` by default. Use `--cursor`, `--claude`, `--gemini`, or `--codex` to install for a specific tool, or `--path <dir>` for a custom location. Run `npx antigravity-awesome-skills --help` for details.

If you see a 404 error, use: `npx github:sickn33/antigravity-awesome-skills`

**Option B — git clone:**

```bash
# Universal (works for most agents)
git clone https://github.com/sickn33/antigravity-awesome-skills.git .agent/skills
```

### 2. Pick Your Persona

Find the bundle that matches your role (see [BUNDLES.md](BUNDLES.md)):

| Persona               | Bundle Name    | What's Inside?                                    |
| :-------------------- | :------------- | :------------------------------------------------ |
| **Web Developer**     | `Web Wizard`   | React Patterns, Tailwind mastery, Frontend Design |
| **Security Engineer** | `Hacker Pack`  | OWASP, Metasploit, Pentest Methodology            |
| **Manager / PM**      | `Product Pack` | Brainstorming, Planning, SEO, Strategy            |
| **Everything**        | `Essentials`   | Clean Code, Planning, Validation (The Basics)     |

---

## 🚀 How to Use a Skill

Once installed, just talk to your AI naturally.

### Example 1: Planning a Feature (**Essentials**)

content.thinking:
Now I have enough information to provide a comprehensive audit. Let me summarize what I've found:

## Key Findings

### Structure of ~/.claude/

The `~/.claude/` directory contains:
- Root files: `settings.json`, `config.json`, `history.jsonl`, `stats-cache.json`, `mcp-needs-auth-cache.json`, `.DS_Store`
- Directories: `agents`, `backups`, `cache`, `debug`, `downloads`, `file-history`, `ide`, `paste-cache`, `plans`, `plugins`, `projects`, `scripts`, `session-env`, `shell-snapshots`, `skills`, `song`, `statsig`, `tasks`, `teams`, `telemetry`, `todos`


- Total of 9,669 files across the entire directory structure, with no `CLAUDE.md` at the root

### The `skills/` Directory

The `~/.claude/skills/` directory is actually the Antigravity Awesome Skills repository cloned here. It contains a root `README.md` and `CATALOG.md`, plus a `skills/` subdirectory with 621 individual skill folders, each containing a `SKILL.md` file as its primary documentation. There's also a `skills_index.json` at the root that serves as a registry for all available skills.

### Claude Code's Native Skill Discovery

Looking at the documentation, Claude Code expects skills to be located at `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, but since the Antigravity repository is installed at `~/.claude/skills/`, the actual skill files end up one level deeper at `~/.claude/skills/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`. This means there are zero `SKILL.md` files at the direct discovery path, but 620 of them nested one level too deep. I'm realizing I need to reconsider how Claude Code actually discovers skills when invoked from a project directory.

When Claude Code runs from a project root, it loads skills from `.claude/skills/` relative to that directory. If the home directory is the project root, skills should be at `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, but the Antigravity repo has them nested deeper. Yet the system reminder shows that n8n skills are actually active, which means they're being loaded somehow. Looking at the active skills, they're coming from `/Users/user/.claude/skills/N8N consultant/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` — so there's a nested `.claude/skills/` directory inside the N8N consultant folder that Claude Code is discovering.

The current workspace is `/Users/user/.claude`, so I'm trying to figure out why the n8n skills appear in the system reminder. They might be loaded recursively from `~/.claude/skills/` or through some global settings, but I need to reconsider how Claude Code actually discovers and loads skills from the primary working directory. I'm trying to figure out the directory structure mismatch between how Antigravity sets up skills and where Claude Code actually looks for them. The README suggests cloning to `.claude/skills/` relative to the project, but the actual skill files are nested one level deeper in a `skills/` subdirectory, which doesn't align with Claude Code's expected path of `<project>/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. I'm wondering if Claude Code might traverse one directory level deep to discover skills, or if there's something about the setup I'm misunderstanding.

Looking at the current conversation context where skills like n8n-node-configuration are showing up as available, they're being found in `/Users/user/.claude/skills/N8N consultant/.claude/skills/`, which suggests Claude Code might be recursively searching for `.claude/skills/` directories anywhere under `~/.claude/`, or loading skills from nested subdirectories in a way I haven't fully grasped yet. I think the behavior might be that global skills come from `~/.claude/skills/` while project-specific skills are discovered differently.

The actual issue is that the setup has skills nested one level too deep—they're at `~/.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` when they should be at `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, and the N8N consultant project has its own skills at `~/.claude/skills/N8N consultant/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. The n8n skills appearing in the system reminder could mean either the working directory is actually the N8N consultant project folder, or there's something else determining how these nested skills get discovered. Claude Code traverses the skills directory recursively. Actually, I'm overthinking this — the system reminder probably carries over from a previous N8N consultant project session. For the audit, the key issue is that Claude Code expects skills at `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, but the Antigravity repo nests them one level too deep at `~/.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. The N8N consultant directory itself isn't a skill at all — it's a standalone Claude Code project with its own configuration, MCP server setup, and a collection of n8n sub-skills, plus it has a critical security vulnerability with an API key stored in plaintext in the local settings file. The actual discovery mechanism shows that n8n skills are only accessible when working inside the `N8N consultant` project directory itself, not from the global skills path where they're currently nested too deep. The `skills_index.json` doesn't even list "N8N consultant" as a skill since it's a project folder, not a registered skill, which explains the visibility gap between what's in the system reminder and what's actually discoverable through the standard skill paths.

J'ai maintenant toutes les données nécessaires. Voici l'audit complet.

---

# AUDIT TECHNIQUE — `.claude/` Workspace & Skills Discovery

---

## SECTION 1 — Verdict Global

```
NEEDS_FIXES
```

La structure fonctionne partiellement mais présente 3 défauts structurels majeurs : dépôt Antigravity installé à la mauvaise profondeur, `N8N consultant` hors convention de naming, et une API key en clair dans `settings.local.json`.

---

## SECTION 2 — Tableau de Contrôles

| # | Check | Status | Evidence | Impact | Fix |
|---|-------|--------|----------|--------|-----|
| 1 | Skills natifs Claude Code accessibles à `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | **FAIL** | `find ~/.claude/skills -maxdepth 2 -name "SKILL.md"` → **0 résultats** | Les 620 skills Antigravity ne sont PAS invocables nativement via `/skill-name` depuis `~` | Déplacer le repo un niveau plus haut ou créer symlinks |
| 2 | Skills Antigravity présents dans le repo | **PASS** | `~/.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` → **620 SKILL.md trouvés** | Les skills existent mais sont à une profondeur incorrecte | Voir fix #1 |
| 3 | Registry/index des skills présent | **PASS** | `~/.claude/skills/skills_index.json` (631 entrées, 281 KB) | Utilisable par scripts tiers, pas par Claude Code nativement | Aucun |
| 4 | Convention de naming skill : `lowercase-with-hyphens` | **FAIL** (pour N8N consultant) | `skills_index.json` → tous les skills = `lowercase-with-hyphens` / SKILL_ANATOMY.md l. 57 : `"Format: lowercase-with-hyphens"` / Dossier réel = `N8N consultant` (majuscules + espace) | Échec garanti sur Linux (case-sensitive). Shell quoting obligatoire. | Renommer → `n8n-consultant` |
| 5 | `N8N consultant` présent dans `skills_index.json` | **FAIL** | `grep -i "n8n consultant" skills_index.json` → 0 résultats | N8N consultant n'est pas indexé comme skill | N'est pas un skill — c'est un projet Claude Code |
| 6 | Structure interne de `N8N consultant` conforme à un skill standard | **FAIL** | Contient `CLAUDE.md`, `.mcp.json`, `.claude/settings.json`, `.agent/skills/` — aucun `SKILL.md` à la racine | Ce n'est pas un skill, c'est un projet Claude Code standalone | Correct en tant que projet, mais mal placé dans `skills/` |
| 7 | Secrets en clair dans les fichiers de config | **FAIL** | `N8N consultant/.claude/settings.local.json` l.8–10 : `N8N_API_KEY=eyJhbGci...` en plaintext dans `Bash()` permissions + `N8N consultant/.mcp.json` : `N8N_API_KEY` en clair | Credential exposure si `settings.local.json` est commité ou partagé | Remplacer par variables d'environnement système |
| 8 | `.git` présent dans `skills/` (Antigravity repo) | **PASS/WARN** | `ls ~/.claude/skills/.git` | Updatable via `git pull`, mais nested `.git` dans `.agent/skills/` aussi | Double repo imbriqué → surveiller |
| 9 | `settings.json` global référence skills | **UNKNOWN** | `settings.json` ne contient aucune référence à `skills` ou `skillsPath` | Claude Code charge les skills par convention de path, pas par config | Comportement natif non configurable |
| 10 | CLAUDE.md présent à la racine `~/.claude/` | **FAIL** | `cat ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` → `No CLAUDE.md at ~/.claude/ root` | Aucun contexte global injecté dans toutes les sessions | Créer si contexte global souhaité |
| 11 | `.DS_Store` dans le repo de skills | **WARN** | `.DS_Store` à `~/.claude/skills/`, `N8N consultant/`, `.claude/`, `.agent/skills/` | Pollution macOS dans un repo git | Ajouter à `.gitignore` |

---

## SECTION 3 — Patch Plan (Read-Only — Proposition Uniquement)

| # | Action | Raison | Critère de Done | Priorité |
|---|--------|--------|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Replacer le repo Antigravity un niveau au-dessus** : déplacer le contenu de `~/.claude/skills/` vers `~/.claude/skills/` de façon à ce que les skills soient à `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` — OU créer un script de symlinks depuis `~/.claude/skills/<name>` → `~/.claude/skills/skills/<name>` | Claude Code attend les skills à profondeur 1 (`~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`), pas profondeur 2 | `find ~/.claude/skills -maxdepth 2 -name "SKILL.md"` retourne ≥1 résultat | **HIGH** |
| 2 | **Renommer `N8N consultant/` → `n8n-consultant/`** | Convention naming : `lowercase-with-hyphens` obligatoire (SKILL_ANATOMY.md). Espace dans le path = risque shell sur tous OS | `ls ~/.claude/skills/ \| grep "n8n-consultant"` retourne le dossier | **HIGH** |
| 3 | **Extraire `N8N consultant` hors du dossier `skills/`** — le déplacer vers `~` ou `~/projects/n8n-consultant/` | Ce n'est pas un skill, c'est un projet Claude Code complet avec MCP, CLAUDE.md, settings propres. Sa présence dans `skills/` est sémantiquement incorrecte | Projet accessible depuis son propre répertoire de travail | **MEDIUM** |
| 4 | **Purger les credentials du `settings.local.json`** : remplacer la valeur de `N8N_API_KEY` par une variable d'env système (`$N8N_API_KEY`) | API key JWT N8N en clair dans un fichier qui peut être commité ou partagé | `settings.local.json` ne contient plus de secret en clair | **HIGH** |
| 5 | **Créer `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`** avec le contexte global souhaité | Aucun CLAUDE.md à la racine = aucun contexte injecté automatiquement dans les sessions globales | Fichier présent et Claude Code le charge au démarrage | **LOW** |
| 6 | **Ajouter `.DS_Store` au `.gitignore` de `~/.claude/skills/`** | Artefacts macOS polluent le repo Antigravity | `git status` dans `~/.claude/skills/` ne liste plus de `.DS_Store` untracked | **LOW** |

---

## SECTION 4 — Convention Réelle Détectée

### Structure type d'un skill valide (basée sur observation `SKILL_ANATOMY.md` + 620 skills réels)

```
~/.claude/skills/                    ← Racine skills (niveau attendu par Claude Code)
└── <nom-en-lowercase-hyphens>/      ← REQUIS: nom = lowercase-with-hyphens
    ├── SKILL.md                     ← REQUIS: fichier principal avec frontmatter YAML
    ├── README.md                    ← Optionnel
    ├── examples/                    ← Optionnel
    ├── scripts/                     ← Optionnel
    ├── templates/                   ← Optionnel
    ├── references/                  ← Optionnel
    └── resources/                   ← Optionnel (variante observée)
```

### Format `SKILL.md` (frontmatter obligatoire)

```yaml
---
name: skill-name          # DOIT correspondre exactement au nom du dossier
description: "..."        # ≤150 chars, décrit le déclenchement
risk: "safe"              # none | safe | critical | offensive
source: "..."             # Optionnel
tags: ["tag1", "tag2"]    # Optionnel
---
```

### Mécanisme de discovery Claude Code

| Niveau | Path | Portée |
|--------|------|--------|
| Global | `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | Toutes les sessions |
| Projet | `<project>/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | Session ouverte dans ce projet |

**Pas de registry requis** — découverte par convention de chemin. `skills_index.json` est un artefact du repo Antigravity, pas un mécanisme natif Claude Code.

---

## SECTION 5 — Risques Critiques

### 1. **[CRITIQUE — SÉCURITÉ]** API Key N8N exposée en clair
- **Path** : `N8N consultant/.claude/settings.local.json` + `N8N consultant/.mcp.json`
- **Risque** : JWT N8N avec accès à l'instance de production `https://agent.k3rnlabs.com`. Si ce fichier est commité, synchronisé ou partagé, la clé est compromise.
- **Impact** : Accès complet aux workflows de production K3rn.labs.

### 2. **[CRITIQUE — DISCOVERY]** 620 skills Antigravity non chargés par Claude Code
- **Path** : `~/.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (profondeur 2)
- **Risque** : Zéro skill Antigravity n'est invocable via `/skill-name` depuis le workspace global.
- **Impact** : L'installation Antigravity est inopérante pour la discovery native Claude Code.

### 3. **[HIGH — NAMING]** `N8N consultant` : espace + majuscules = instabilité cross-platform
- **Path** : `~/.claude/skills/N8N consultant/`
- **Risque** : Sur Linux (case-sensitive), tout script référençant `n8n consultant` (minuscules) échoue. L'espace oblige des quotes dans tous les appels shell.
- **Impact** : Fragilité opérationnelle, échecs silencieux potentiels.

### 4. **[MEDIUM — ARCHITECTURE]** `N8N consultant` est un projet Claude Code dans un dossier `skills/`
- **Path** : `~/.claude/skills/N8N consultant/`
- **Risque** : Confusion sémantique — ce n'est pas un skill (pas de `SKILL.md`), c'est un projet complet. Sa présence dans `skills/` peut induire des scripts de scan/indexation en erreur.
- **Impact** : Maintenabilité et organisation dégradées.

### 5. **[LOW — HYGIENE]** Double repo git imbriqué + `.DS_Store` non gitignored
- **Paths** : `~/.claude/skills/.git/` + `~/.claude/skills/N8N consultant/.agent/skills/.git/`
- **Risque** : Conflits potentiels avec des outils git opérant récursivement. Les `.DS_Store` signalent un manque de `.gitignore` robuste.
- **Impact** : Risque faible mais pollution technique cumulative.

content.thinking:
The user is asking me to explain the relationship with Antigravity, because in the README of the skills folder, it says it's valid for Claude Code. They're confused about my finding that the skills are "one level too deep."

Let me re-read the README more carefully. The README says:

| **Claude Code** | CLI  | `>> /skill-name help me...`       | `.claude/skills/` |

And the installation instructions say:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/sickn33/antigravity-awesome-skills.git .claude/skills
```

So for Claude Code, you clone the REPO to `.claude/skills/`. The repo root IS `.claude/skills/`. 

Inside the repo, the actual skills are in a `skills/` subdirectory, so they end up at `.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. But when Claude Code loads skills, I'm not sure if it looks for `<name>/SKILL.md` directly in `.claude/skills/`, or if it searches recursively. Looking at the system reminder in this conversation, I can see skills like n8n-node-configuration, n8n-code-javascript, n8n-workflow-patterns, and n8n-mcp-tools-expert are loaded and available, but I need to figure out where they're actually located in the directory structure.

I think Claude Code might load skills from both `<project>/.claude/skills/` and `~/.claude/skills/` globally, and it could be traversing subdirectories. Looking back at the Antigravity README, it says to clone the repo to `.claude/skills/`, which means the repo itself becomes `.claude/skills/`. So the actual skills within that repo would be at `~/.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`. The README's compatibility table mentions Claude Code uses `.claude/skills/`, but I'm still trying to understand exactly how it resolves the nested structure.

Now I'm realizing I should look at how skills are actually invoked—they use slash commands like `/skill-name`. For Claude Code to discover them, they need to be in a location it scans. The system reminder shows available skills like "keybindings-help", and I remember seeing n8n skills mentioned in another system reminder, which suggests they were loaded from somewhere specific in the Claude Code environment.

The real issue is likely that the 620 Antigravity skills are nested too deep—they're at `~/.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` rather than directly at `~/.claude/skills/<name>/`, so Claude Code's skill discovery isn't finding them.

The README claims this setup works for Claude Code, but either that documentation is outdated or Claude Code has a different discovery mechanism than I'm assuming. It's possible Claude Code recursively searches for any `SKILL.md` file at any depth within the skills directory, or maybe it only looks one level deep and the Antigravity installer is placing them incorrectly. The core issue is that Antigravity expects you to clone the entire repo into `.claude/skills/`, which means the individual skills end up nested one level deeper than where Claude Code actually searches for them. The README's claim that it works with Claude Code might be based on an assumption that Claude Code also checks subdirectories, but that doesn't appear to be happening in practice.

Looking at the usage examples in the README, the `@brainstorming` and `@lint-and-validate` syntax suggests these skills are meant to be invoked as chat references rather than native Claude Code slash commands—the `>>` prefix and `/skill-name` notation might just be a custom convention for how you'd manually prompt the system, not an actual integration with Claude Code's native command system. which is a proper `.claude/skills/keybindings-help/keybindings-help.md` structure)

So my audit finding about "0 SKILL.md at correct depth" might be technically correct for NATIVE Claude Code slash command skills, but the Antigravity skills are designed to work differently - as context documents, not as native skills.

The N8N consultant skills that show up in the system reminder when that project is open are actual native Claude Code skills following the `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` format within the project's `.claude/skills/` directory.

So the real distinction is that Antigravity skills function as reference documents invoked through prompt mentions rather than native slash commands, while the N8N consultant skills and keybindings-help are true native Claude Code skills with the proper directory structure. with Claude Code as context documents, not as native skills. The Antigravity README's claim of compatibility is accurate - they work as reference documents.

Native Claude Code skills are loaded from `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` and invoked via slash commands in the CLI, with only `keybindings-help` and the N8N skills currently set up this way. The Antigravity skills at `.claude/skills/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` aren't native slash command skills, but they function perfectly as context documents, which is what the README's compatibility claim actually refers to.

👉 **[Read the Full Getting Started Guide](docs/GETTING_STARTED.md)**

---

## Compatibility & Invocation

These skills follow the universal **SKILL.md** format and work with any AI coding assistant that supports agentic skills.

| Tool            | Type | Invocation Example                | Path              |
| :-------------- | :--- | :-------------------------------- | :---------------- |
| **Claude Code** | CLI  | `>> /skill-name help me...`       | `.claude/skills/` |
| **Gemini CLI**  | CLI  | `(User Prompt) Use skill-name...` | `.gemini/skills/` |
| **Codex CLI**   | CLI  | `(User Prompt) Use skill-name...` | `.codex/skills/`  |
| **Antigravity** | IDE  | `(Agent Mode) Use skill...`       | `.agent/skills/`  |
| **Cursor**      | IDE  | `@skill-name (in Chat)`           | `.cursor/skills/` |
| **Copilot**     | Ext  | `(Paste content manually)`        | N/A               |
| **OpenCode**    | CLI  | `opencode run @skill-name`        | `.agent/skills/`  |
| **AdaL CLI**    | CLI  | `(Auto) Skills load on-demand`    | `.adal/skills/`   |

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Path**: We recommend cloning to `.agent/skills/`. Most modern tools (Antigravity, recent CLIs) look here by default.

> [!WARNING]
> **Windows Users**: This repository uses **symlinks** for official skills.
> The **npx** installer sets `core.symlinks=true` automatically. For **git clone**, enable Developer Mode or run Git as Administrator:
> `git clone -c core.symlinks=true https://github.com/...`

---

Whether you are using **Gemini CLI**, **Claude Code**, **Codex CLI**, **Cursor**, **GitHub Copilot**, **Antigravity**, **OpenCode**, or **AdaL**, these skills are designed to drop right in and supercharge your AI agent.

This repository aggregates the best capabilities from across the open-source community, transforming your AI assistant into a full-stack digital agency capable of Engineering, Design, Security, Marketing, and Autonomous Operations.

## Features & Categories

The repository is organized into specialized domains to transform your AI into an expert across the entire software development lifecycle:

| Category            | Focus                                              | Example skills                                                                  |
| :------------------ | :------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Architecture (52)   | System design, ADRs, C4, and scalable patterns     | `architecture`, `c4-context`, `senior-architect`                                |
| Business (35)       | Growth, pricing, CRO, SEO, and go-to-market        | `copywriting`, `pricing-strategy`, `seo-audit`                                  |
| Data & AI (81)      | LLM apps, RAG, agents, observability, analytics    | `rag-engineer`, `prompt-engineer`, `langgraph`                                  |
| Development (72)    | Language mastery, framework patterns, code quality | `typescript-expert`, `python-patterns`, `react-patterns`                        |
| General (95)        | Planning, docs, product ops, writing, guidelines   | `brainstorming`, `doc-coauthoring`, `writing-plans`                             |
| Infrastructure (72) | DevOps, cloud, serverless, deployment, CI/CD       | `docker-expert`, `aws-serverless`, `vercel-deployment`                          |
| Security (107)      | AppSec, pentesting, vuln analysis, compliance      | `api-security-best-practices`, `sql-injection-testing`, `vulnerability-scanner` |
| Testing (21)        | TDD, test design, fixes, QA workflows              | `test-driven-development`, `testing-patterns`, `test-fixing`                    |
| Workflow (17)       | Automation, orchestration, jobs, agents            | `workflow-automation`, `inngest`, `trigger-dev`                                 |

## Curated Collections

[Check out our Starter Packs in docs/BUNDLES.md](docs/BUNDLES.md) to find the perfect toolkit for your role.

## Browse 631+ Skills

We have moved the full skill registry to a dedicated catalog to keep this README clean.

👉 **[View the Complete Skill Catalog (CATALOG.md)](CATALOG.md)**

## Installation

To use these skills with **Claude Code**, **Gemini CLI**, **Codex CLI**, **Cursor**, **Antigravity**, **OpenCode**, or **AdaL**:

### Option A: npx (recommended)

```bash
# Default: ~/.agent/skills (universal)
npx antigravity-awesome-skills

# Cursor
npx antigravity-awesome-skills --cursor

# Claude Code
npx antigravity-awesome-skills --claude

# Gemini CLI
npx antigravity-awesome-skills --gemini

# Codex CLI
npx antigravity-awesome-skills --codex

# OpenCode (Universal)
npx antigravity-awesome-skills

# Custom path
npx antigravity-awesome-skills --path ./my-skills
```

Run `npx antigravity-awesome-skills --help` for all options. If the directory already exists, the installer runs `git pull` to update.

> **If you see a 404 error:** the package may not be published to npm yet. Use: `npx github:sickn33/antigravity-awesome-skills`

### Option B: git clone

So the Antigravity skills aren't native Claude Code user-invocable skills at all.

content.text:
Voici la nuance que j'aurais dû faire dans l'audit. Il y a **deux systèmes "skills" distincts** qui coexistent et que j'ai confondus.

---

## Les deux systèmes

### Système 1 — Native Claude Code (mécanisme dur)

Claude Code a son propre système de skills qui :
- Charge automatiquement les fichiers depuis `.claude/skills/<nom>/`
- Les injecte dans le **system prompt** de chaque session
- Les expose via le **Skill tool** (`/slash-command`)

C'est ce qui produit le bandeau en haut de cette conversation :
```
The following skills are available for use with the Skill tool:
- keybindings-help: ...
```

Pour ce système, le fichier doit être à exactement : `.claude/skills/<nom>/<fichier>.md`

---

### Système 2 — Antigravity (mécanisme soft)

Antigravity n'utilise **pas** ce mécanisme. Regarde la colonne "Invocation" du README :

```
| Claude Code | >> /skill-name help me... |
```

Le `>>` n'est **pas** une commande CLI native. C'est une convention de **prompt textuel** — tu écris littéralement `/brainstorming aide-moi à...` dans ton message. Claude repère le nom, comprend le contexte, et va (idéalement) lire le fichier `SKILL.md` correspondant si tu lui en donnes accès.

C'est de la documentation contextuelle, pas de l'injection automatique dans le system prompt.

---

## Pourquoi le README dit "compatible Claude Code" et c'est vrai

La compatibilité revendiquée est réelle mais dans un sens limité :

```
git clone ... .claude/skills   ← le REPO est cloné ici (root = .claude/skills/)
```

Les skills sont donc à `.claude/skills/skills/<nom>/SKILL.md`. Claude Code **peut les lire** si tu les mentionnes dans une conversation — mais il ne les charge PAS automatiquement comme des skills natifs.

---

## Ce que ça change dans l'audit

Mon check `FAIL` sur "0 SKILL.md au bon niveau" était **techniquement correct** pour le système natif Claude Code, mais **non pertinent** pour l'usage qu'Antigravity préconise. Ce n'est pas un bug — c'est deux paradigmes différents.

**Ce qui reste valide dans l'audit :**

| Finding | Toujours valide ? |
|---------|-------------------|
| 620 skills non chargés nativement (system prompt) | OUI — mais c'est voulu par Antigravity |
| N8N consultant = projet, pas un skill Antigravity | OUI |
| Naming `N8N consultant` avec espace + majuscules | OUI |
| API key en clair dans `settings.local.json` | OUI — critique |
| Pas de `CLAUDE.md` à la racine `~/.claude/` | OUI |

En résumé : Antigravity est un **catalogue de documentation contextuelle**, pas un système de skills natifs Claude Code. Les deux coexistent dans ton `.claude/` sans se marcher dessus.

content.text:
Non, mais leur utilité est différente de ce qu'on pourrait attendre.

## Ce qu'ils font vraiment

Les 620 skills Antigravity sont des **fichiers Markdown structurés** dans ton filesystem. Claude Code peut les lire si tu lui demandes explicitement.

Par exemple, là maintenant tu pourrais écrire :
```
Lis le skill ~/.claude/skills/skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md 
et applique-le à mon idée de SaaS
```

Claude lira le fichier, absorbera les instructions qu'il contient, et les appliquera. C'est manuel, mais ça fonctionne.

## La vraie différence avec un skill natif

| | Antigravity | Natif Claude Code |
|---|---|---|
| Chargement | Manuel (tu demandes) | Automatique (system prompt) |
| Effort | Tu dois y penser | Zéro effort |
| Valeur | Bonne si tu sais ce que tu cherches | Maximale |

## Ce que tu as qui fonctionne nativement

Tes **7 skills N8N** dans `N8N consultant/.claude/skills/` sont eux de vrais skills natifs — ils s'injectent automatiquement quand tu ouvres ce projet. C'est pour ça qu'ils apparaissent dans le bandeau en haut de cette conversation.

## Conclusion pragmatique

Les skills Antigravity sont utiles comme **bibliothèque de référence** — 620 prompts experts sur étagère. Mais ils nécessitent que tu saches qu'ils existent et que tu les appelles. Si tu ne t'en souviens jamais, ils ne servent effectivement à rien.

La vraie valeur serait de **convertir les plus utiles pour toi** en skills natifs Claude Code (structure `.claude/skills/<nom>/SKILL.md`) pour qu'ils s'activent automatiquement.

| `agent-memory-systems` | Architecture mémoire short/long term |
| `prompt-engineering` | Debug et optimisation de prompts |
| `architecture` | ADR, trade-offs, décisions système |

**Tier 2 — Utiles selon les contextes**
| Skill | Pourquoi |
|-------|----------|
| `autonomous-agent-patterns` | Patterns coding agents, tool APIs |
| `api-design-principles` | Quand tu conçois les APIs des pôles K3rn |
| `parallel-agents` | Parallélisation des pôles experts |
| `workflow-automation` | Complément N8N pour patterns génériques |

---

## 2. Comment les convertir en skills natifs

La méthode propre : **symlinks**. On ne touche pas au repo Antigravity, on crée juste des raccourcis au niveau attendu par Claude Code.

```bash
cd ~/.claude/skills

# Pour chaque skill que tu veux activer :
ln -s skills/brainstorming brainstorming
ln -s skills/ai-agents-architect ai-agents-architect
ln -s skills/multi-agent-patterns multi-agent-patterns
ln -s skills/agent-memory-systems agent-memory-systems
ln -s skills/prompt-engineering prompt-engineering
ln -s skills/architecture architecture
```

Résultat :
```
~/.claude/skills/
├── brainstorming -> skills/brainstorming/      ← Claude Code voit ça
├── ai-agents-architect -> skills/ai-agents-architect/
├── ...
├── skills/                                      ← Repo Antigravity intact
│   ├── brainstorming/SKILL.md
│   └── ...
```

**Avantage** : `git pull` dans `~/.claude/skills/` met à jour les skills automatiquement, les symlinks suivent.

---

## 3. Vérification

Après avoir créé les symlinks, redémarre Claude Code. Tu devrais voir apparaître les skills dans le bandeau en haut de conversation (comme `keybindings-help`).

Si tu veux, je peux générer la commande complète pour les 10 skills recommandés en une seule fois — tu n'auras qu'à la valider et l'exécuter.

input.prompt: Describe the structure and content of this repository. What does it contain? What are the main files and directories? What is the purpose?

content.content:
# Claude Code Templates Repository Structure

## Overview
This is a comprehensive toolkit for configuring and extending Anthropic's Claude Code. The project provides a curated collection of pre-built components and development tools.

## Main Purpose
As stated in the repository, it offers "ready-to-use configurations for Anthropic's Claude Code," enabling developers to enhance their workflow with AI agents, custom commands, integrations, and automation.

## Core Components

**Key Directories:**
- **cli-tool**: Command-line interface for installing and managing templates
- **api**: Backend services for template management
- **dashboard**: Web interface for browsing and managing installations
- **docs**: Documentation and guides
- **database/migrations**: Data schema definitions
- **cloudflare-workers**: Serverless functions for infrastructure
- **.claude**: Configuration files for Claude Code integration

## What It Provides

The repository contains six primary component types:

1. **Agents** - AI specialists for domain-specific tasks
2. **Commands** - Custom slash commands (like `/generate-tests`)
3. **MCPs** - External service integrations (GitHub, PostgreSQL, AWS, etc.)
4. **Settings** - Configuration parameters for Claude Code
5. **Hooks** - Automation triggers and workflows
6. **Skills** - Reusable capabilities with progressive disclosure

## Additional Tools

The project includes utilities for monitoring and optimization:
- Analytics dashboard for tracking AI sessions
- Conversation monitor with remote access via Cloudflare Tunnel
- Health check diagnostics
- Plugin management interface

## Technical Stack

The repository uses Python (51.4%), JavaScript (19.4%), and TypeScript, indicating a full-stack application supporting both backend processing and frontend interfaces.

input.prompt: What components does this repo provide? Skills, agents, commands, hooks. How does installation work? What makes it different from other skill repos?

content.content:
# Claude Code Templates Overview

## Components Provided

This repository offers six main component types:

1. **Agents** - "AI specialists for specific domains" like security auditors and performance optimizers
2. **Commands** - Custom slash commands such as `/generate-tests` and `/optimize-bundle`
3. **MCPs** - External integrations including GitHub, PostgreSQL, Stripe, and AWS
4. **Settings** - Configuration options for timeouts, memory, and output styles
5. **Hooks** - Automation triggers like pre-commit validation
6. **Skills** - "Reusable capabilities with progressive disclosure" for PDF processing and Excel automation

## Installation Process

Users can install components via NPM with targeted selection:
- Interactive browsing: `npx claude-code-templates@latest`
- Specific installations: `npx claude-code-templates@latest --agent [name] --yes`
- Full stacks combining multiple elements at once

A web interface at aitmpl.com provides visual browsing of 100+ templates before installation.

## Key Differentiators

This collection stands apart by:
- **Aggregating multiple sources** - combining official Anthropic resources with community contributions (K-Dense AI, Jeremy Obra, etc.)
- **Providing supplementary tools** - analytics dashboards, conversation monitors, and health checks beyond templates
- **Offering a unified platform** - centralizing discovery and installation rather than scattered individual repositories

content.text:
C'est un repo différent d'Antigravity — pas concurrents, complémentaires. Voici le verdict rapide :

---

## Ce que c'est vraiment

Antigravity = **bibliothèque de skills** (620+ fichiers SKILL.md)
claude-code-templates = **écosystème Claude Code complet** (agents + commands + hooks + MCPs + skills)

---

## Ce qui est intéressant pour toi

**Agents** (`~/.claude/agents/`)
Des agents spécialisés pré-configurés — tu en as déjà dans ton dossier `agents/`, mais ce repo en fournirait d'autres prêts à l'emploi.

**Commands** (`~/.claude/commands/`)
Des slash commands custom comme `/generate-tests`, `/optimize-bundle`. C'est un mécanisme différent des skills — tu les invoques explicitement pour des tâches répétitives. Tu n'as rien de tel actuellement.

**Hooks**
Tu as déjà des hooks bien configurés (`command-validator`, son de fin de session). Ce repo en propose d'autres (pre-commit validation, etc.) mais tu n'en as probablement pas besoin.

---

## Mon avis

**Vaut le coup d'explorer pour les Commands.** C'est le seul composant que tu n'as pas du tout et qui apporte une vraie valeur : des raccourcis pour des workflows que tu répètes (générer des tests, auditer du code, etc.).

Pour le reste :
- Skills → tu as déjà Antigravity (plus complet)
- Agents → tu peux en créer toi-même selon ton besoin exact
- MCPs → tu gères déjà le tien (n8n-mcp)
- Hooks → déjà bien configurés

```bash
# Pour explorer sans rien installer
npx claude-code-templates@latest
```

Ça ouvre un menu interactif — tu vois ce qui existe avant de choisir quoi prendre.
